Plaintiff'S Motion For Issue Sanctions, Evidentiary Sanctions, Or Terminating Sanctions. (Per Ex Parte Ost Order Filed 1-13-20)
Off Calendar Jan-21-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Nestor R. Schnasse, Esq. of STRICK LAW OFFICES, T:415.721.1200, for plaintiff/moving party;
Bradley D. Doucette, Esq. of COLLINS COLLINS MUIR + STEWART LLP, T:510.844.5100, for defendant/opposing party.

RULING: Hearing held on Plaintiff'S Motion For Issue Sanctions, Evidentiary Sanctions, Or Terminating Sanctions.

Plaintiff's counsel brought 2 issues before the Court: 1) That defendant stipulate to the authenticity of certain photograph(s) produced; and 2) That deposition dates for Mr. Nibbi and Mr. Narvack be set.

Defendant's counsel stated that he could not stipulate to the photograph's authenticity; only his client can. And the depositions of Mr. Nibbi and Mr. Narvack have been set for January 23, 2020 at 10am and 1pm.

After hearing, the Court did not rule on the motion for sanctions.

The Court also told the parties to do a better job of communicating and, if needed, to confirm things in writing.

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT .
Off Calendar Jan-08-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Lawrence Strick, Esq. 415-721-1200 for Plaintiff.
Nadim Hegazi, Esq. 510-844-5100 of Collins Collins Muir & Stewart for Defendant.

RULING - A R G U E D. Defendant argues. Plaintiff is satisifed with the tentative ruling. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT NIBBI BROS. ASSOCIATES, INC.'s MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Defendant Nibbi Brothers Construction's motion for summary judgment is denied. Nibbi maintains its initial summary judgment burden under Alvarez v. Seaside Transportation Services LLC (2017) 13 Cal.App.5th 635, 641-642 [independent contractor's hirer maintained summary judgment burden based on the Privette presumption, which establishes a presumption that the hirer delegated the duty to provide a safe work environment to the hired contractor]. However, Plaintiff demonstrates a triable issue of material fact regarding whether Nibbi's direct negligence affirmatively contributed to her injury. (See Hooker v. Department of Transportation (2002) 27 Cal.4th 198, 202; Browne v. Turner Const. Co. (2005) 127 Cal.App.4th 1334, 1344-1345 ["the hirer can only be liable where it injures a worker through its own negligence." (emphasis in original)] [reversing summary judgment where issue of fact existed whether general contractor affirmatively contributed to subcontractor's employee's injury].) The evidence demonstrates that: (1) Nibbi created the skylight that plaintiff fell through (Sly Depo., 30:15-31:8) and (2) Nibbi removed the shoring that was underneath the skylight. (Sanchez Depo., 34:16-19.) A reasonable trier of fact could conclude that Nibbi's affirmative conduct was a substantial factor in causing plaintiff's injury.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)
